first_aid: Remove stinger, wash, cold compress, antihistamine/analgesic; epinephrine if anaphylaxis
hospital_importance: visit hospital only if:breathing difficulty, severe swelling, stinger in eye or throat, Otherwise: home care is enough

venom_type: Cytotoxic Venom. Stinger NOT typically left behind
symptoms: sharp, intense pain. Redness, swelling and itching
severity_level: Worst: Anaphylaxis or multiple stings causing systemic toxicity
first_aid: Remove stinger (if present), cold compress, oral antihistamine, analgesic; seek urgent care if systemic signs
hospital_importance: 🚨 Urgent emergency care — high venom toxicity + swelling of airway

symptoms: This is the scientific order for bees, wasps, ants. Symptoms are the same as above

hospital_importance: hospital if systemic symptoms

symptoms: often painless initially. Redness and a "bullseye" pattern may appear
severity_level: Worst: Necrosis, infection, rare systemic hemolysis
first_aid: Clean wound, cold pack, keep elevated; seek medical assessment early to monitor necrosis and wound care. Avoid unnecessary early surgery; antibiotics if secondary infection
hospital_importance: 🏥 Seek hospital — necrosis risk, requires monitoring

venom_type: Cytotoxic Venom. Stinger NOT typically left behind
symptoms: sharp, intense pain. Redness, swelling and itching
reaction_stage: Immediate; delayed swelling may peak 24–48 hours
first_aid: Remove stinger if present (yellow jackets do not leave stinger usually), cold compress, antihistamines, topical steroids for large local reaction; seek care if breathing difficulties
hospital_importance: hospital if large local reaction or multiple stings
